title: Adopt a written decision record process for engineering
context: Contoso Logistics grew from 40 to 220 engineers in two years. Key architectural choices were made in meetings and chat threads, and the reasoning was lost when people changed teams or left. New hires repeatedly re-litigated settled questions.
decision: Every significant technical or process decision must be captured as a numbered decision record (DR) in the shared decision store, including context, the decision, rationale, and alternatives considered.
rationale: Written records preserve institutional memory, shorten onboarding, and prevent decision churn. The lightweight ADR format was chosen over heavyweight RFC processes to keep adoption friction low.
alternativesConsidered: Wiki pages without a fixed template
Full RFC process with mandatory review boards
Do nothing and rely on tribal knowledge

title: Run all new workloads on Azure Kubernetes Service
context: Services were deployed across a mix of VMs, App Service plans, and one self-managed Kubernetes cluster, multiplying operational runbooks and on-call load.
decision: All new services deploy to the shared Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) platform with the standard Helm chart scaffold. Existing services migrate opportunistically.
rationale: A single orchestration platform concentrates operational expertise, enables a shared observability stack, and lets the platform team offer golden paths instead of bespoke support.

title: Standardize on PostgreSQL for transactional services
context: Teams were independently choosing databases (MySQL, MongoDB, Cosmos DB, SQL Server), which fragmented backup tooling, capacity planning, and on-call expertise. Two incidents in Q1 2023 were prolonged because no one on call knew the affected datastore well.
decision: PostgreSQL (Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server) is the default and only approved database for new transactional services. Exceptions require a written decision record approved by the Service Architecture Board.
rationale: PostgreSQL covers relational and document needs (JSONB) for our workloads, and a single engine concentrates expertise, tooling, and incident response. Managed flexible server keeps operational burden low.
alternativesConsidered: Let each team choose its own database

title: TypeScript is the standard language for backend services
context: Backend services were written in Node.js (JavaScript), Python, and some C#. Hiring and internal mobility suffered because skills did not transfer between teams.
decision: New backend services are written in TypeScript on Node.js. Python remains approved for data science and ML workloads; C# remains approved for the legacy ERP integration domain.
rationale: TypeScript matched the existing frontend skill base, static types reduced production defect rates in the pilot teams, and one primary language simplifies shared libraries and code review.

title: All services emit OpenTelemetry traces, metrics, and logs
context: Incident investigations crossed three logging products and two tracing products. Correlating a request across services was manual and slow.
decision: Every service must instrument with OpenTelemetry SDKs and export to the central collector. Proprietary vendor SDKs are not allowed in service code.
rationale: Vendor-neutral instrumentation decouples us from any single observability vendor and gives uniform trace propagation across the fleet.
alternativesConsidered: Standardize on a single vendor SDK

title: Trunk-based development with feature flags
context: Long-lived release branches caused painful merges and three-week release cycles. Hotfixes had to be cherry-picked across branches.
decision: All repositories use trunk-based development: short-lived branches merged to main within two days, releases cut from main, incomplete work hidden behind feature flags.
rationale: Small frequent merges reduce integration risk, and feature flags decouple deployment from release, enabling progressive rollout.

title: Customer PII must remain in the EU data residency boundary
context: Contoso Logistics serves EU freight customers under GDPR and contractual data residency clauses. An audit found one analytics pipeline copying customer records to a US region.
decision: All systems that store or process customer personally identifiable information (PII) must run in EU regions (West Europe / North Europe). Cross-region replication of PII outside the EU is prohibited, including for analytics, backups, and AI model training.
rationale: GDPR exposure and explicit customer contract clauses make non-EU PII storage a legal and commercial risk that outweighs any infrastructure convenience.

title: Adopt Renovate for automated dependency updates
context: Dependency upgrades were ad hoc. A critical vulnerability in a transitive dependency stayed unpatched in four services for six weeks.
decision: All repositories enable Renovate with the shared org preset: weekly grouped updates, immediate PRs for security advisories, automerge for patch updates with passing CI.
rationale: Automated, continuous small updates are cheaper and safer than rare big-bang upgrades, and security advisories get a guaranteed response path.

title: REST over gRPC for partner-facing APIs
context: Freight partners integrate with Contoso APIs from very heterogeneous stacks, including legacy ERP systems that cannot speak HTTP/2 or protobuf.
decision: Partner-facing APIs are REST/JSON with OpenAPI specifications. gRPC is allowed only for internal service-to-service calls.
rationale: REST maximizes partner reach and tooling familiarity; the performance benefits of gRPC do not matter at partner integration volumes.
alternativesConsidered: gRPC with a REST transcoding gateway

title: Standardize on Azure Service Bus for asynchronous messaging
context: Order, fleet, and billing domains each ran their own message broker (RabbitMQ, Kafka, Azure Storage Queues), with three different dead-letter and retry conventions.
decision: Azure Service Bus is the standard broker for inter-service asynchronous messaging. Kafka remains approved solely for the high-volume telemetry ingestion pipeline.
rationale: Service Bus covers our ordering, dead-lettering, and scheduled delivery needs as a managed service; one broker means one set of reliability patterns to teach and monitor.

title: Production access requires just-in-time elevation via PIM
context: Standing admin access to production subscriptions was held by 31 engineers. SOC 2 audit flagged this as a material finding.
decision: No standing production access. Engineers elevate through Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management with a stated reason, a maximum four-hour window, and automatic audit logging.
rationale: Just-in-time access shrinks the blast radius of credential theft and satisfies the SOC 2 finding with minimal workflow friction.

title: GraphQL federation gateway for partner-facing APIs
context: Partner integrations grew from 14 to 90 partners. Partners complained about chattiness: building one shipment dashboard required calls to six REST endpoints. Larger partners now have modern stacks and asked for a query interface. This revisits DR-009.
decision: Partner-facing APIs move to a federated GraphQL gateway. Existing REST endpoints remain available behind the gateway for a 24-month deprecation window. This supersedes DR-009.
rationale: Federation lets domain teams own their subgraphs while partners get one endpoint and fetch exactly the data they need, cutting integration round-trips dramatically.
alternativesConsidered: Keep REST and add bespoke aggregation endpoints

title: LLM features must use Azure OpenAI within the EU boundary
context: Product teams started prototyping with various public LLM APIs. One prototype sent shipment manifests containing customer names to a non-EU endpoint, conflicting with our data residency commitments (see DR-007).
decision: All LLM-powered features must call Azure OpenAI deployments hosted in EU regions through the shared AI gateway, which enforces prompt logging, PII redaction, and rate limits. Direct calls to public LLM APIs from production code are prohibited.
rationale: A single gateway gives us residency compliance, cost attribution, abuse monitoring, and a place to enforce safety policies, while still letting teams ship AI features quickly.
alternativesConsidered: Allowlist multiple LLM vendors per team

title: Monorepo per business domain, not per team
context: After two reorgs, repository ownership no longer matched team structure: 60+ small repos had unclear owners and divergent CI setups.
decision: Code is organized into one monorepo per business domain (orders, fleet, billing, partners, platform). Repos map to long-lived domains rather than transient team shapes.
rationale: Domains are more stable than org charts. Domain monorepos enable atomic cross-service refactors within a domain and shared CI baselines, without the tooling cost of a single company-wide monorepo.
